metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the origin-guard header value is a randomly-generated secret (e.g. a 32-byte hex token), stored in an SSM SecureString (e.g. `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`), fetched by `fetch-secrets.sh` into the compose `.env`, and injected into the nginx config template via `envsubst` — mirroring exactly how `REVIEW_BOT_PORT` and the OAuth creds are handled — so it is never committed to git and the double-lock boundary is cryptographically sound.
findings.finding: The origin-guard header value — the sole mechanism preventing direct-to-origin bypass of the SigV4 gate — has no specified provisioning path into the nginx config. The plan says the compose service environment includes 'the origin-guard header value' and nginx 'requires the origin-guard header,' but it does not state whether the value is generated, stored in SSM as a secret, or how it reaches the nginx config template at deploy time. If an implementer treats it as a static value committed to the nginx config or compose file, the double-lock security model collapses: any caller who reads the git repo can then forge the guard header and reach the opcert handler without SigV4 authentication.
findings.location: What section — 'nginx route /opcert/ → the service that rejects requests missing the origin-guard header'; AC3 — 'the nginx route requires the origin-guard header'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to `ssm.tf`'s `rebar_secret_params` list; add a `get_param opcert-origin-guard` line to `fetch-secrets.sh`; extend `rebar.conf.template` with `if ($http_x_rebar_origin_guard != "${OPCERT_ORIGIN_GUARD}") { return 403; }` in the `/opcert/` location block; substitute `OPCERT_ORIGIN_GUARD` alongside `REVIEW_BOT_PORT` in `compose-up.sh`'s envsubst call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the opcert IAM approach with the established single-owner pattern: confirm that the existing `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` policy (covering `/rebar/prod/*`) already grants the required SSM read, add an `iam_opcert.tf` documentation-anchor file (mirroring `iam_s2.tf`/`iam_s4a.tf`) that records 'no new grant needed — the opcert key sits under the existing prefix,' and update AC1 to assert this documented decision rather than asserting a new policy resource.
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding 'an instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter' for the opcert Ed25519 key, but the existing `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` inline policy (iam.tf) already grants `ssm:GetParameter[s]` on `arn:aws:ssm:…:parameter/rebar/prod/*`, which covers `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`. Adding a redundant narrower inline policy is inconsistent with the established single-owner pattern (iam_s2.tf, iam_s4a.tf explicitly declare no new grant when existing coverage suffices) and creates a misleading impression of tighter scoping that does not actually restrict anything — the instance can still read all `/rebar/prod/*` params via the existing policy.
findings.location: What section — 'an instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter path'; AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that HTTP API (V2) has no resource-based policy mechanism; state explicitly that the 'restricted to a named admin role' control is enforced via an IAM identity policy on the calling role (not a Terraform resource on the API), and specify where that policy will be declared or verified.
findings.finding: API Gateway HTTP API (V2) does not support resource-based policies, so 'invoke permission restricted to a named admin role' cannot be enforced at the API level in Terraform (unlike REST API which has aws_api_gateway_rest_api_policy). For HTTP API IAM auth, restricting which IAM role may call the endpoint must be done via IAM identity policies on the calling principal — a configuration that lives outside the API's own Terraform resources. The plan does not clarify this, leaving the executor without a concrete mechanism for the stated restriction.
findings.location: What section — 'invoke permission restricted to a named admin role over TLS'; AC1 — 'invoke permission restricted to a named admin role'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC4 and the 'Deploy + prove' section to use `added_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log position, stamped via `rebar trusted-env` helper) instead of `added_at_commit` (main git SHA), aligning with the parent epic's authoritative Option B era-at-storage-anchor resolution.
findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 and plan text pin the deployed environment key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` using `added_at_commit` (an explicit git SHA on main), which is the superseded era schema. The parent's authoritative final design resolution (security finding #5, Option B era-at-storage-anchor, the last substantive comment on the epic) replaced this with `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions operator-stamped via `rebar trusted-env` helper). The leaf diverges from the parent by implementing the old schema that the parent explicitly superseded.
findings.location: Leaf AC4: 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol'; leaf What section: 'pin the environment's public key + `env_id` into `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` per the epic's era protocol'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' in AC4 with 'with `added_at_log_position` (the current tickets-branch tip, stamped by `rebar trusted-env add`) per the parent epic's era-at-storage-anchor resolution (Option B, security finding #5)'. Update the 'Deploy + prove' narrative step to call `rebar trusted-env add` to stamp the log position rather than manually recording a main-branch SHA.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the origin-guard header value as a secret stored in SSM Parameter Store (SecureString), fetched at runtime by `fetch-secrets.sh` into the compose `.env` (never committed in plaintext), and exclude it from the API Gateway Terraform integration block's literal values — analogous to how other `/rebar/prod/*` secrets are handled.
findings.finding: The origin-guard header value — the sole mechanism preventing direct-to-origin bypass of SigV4 authentication at the nginx layer — is described as an environment variable in the compose service, but the plan does not declare whether it is treated as a secret (SSM SecureString, never in plaintext IaC/compose) or a plain config value. If it is committed in plaintext, any reader of the compose file or IaC can construct requests that bypass API Gateway entirely.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header value'; AC line 3 — 'the nginx route requires the origin-guard header'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard-header` to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `ssm.tf` (or the new SSM block for this story), fetch it via `fetch-secrets.sh` like the other SecureStrings, and inject it into the compose service as `REBAR_OPCERT_ORIGIN_GUARD` from `.env` — never hardcoded in `docker-compose.yml` or Terraform.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the new `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` SSM parameter does NOT need a separate instance-profile grant — the existing `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` policy on `iam.tf` already covers `parameter/rebar/prod/*`; follow the `iam_s4a.tf` pattern of documenting 'no new IAM needed' rather than adding a redundant narrower statement.
findings.finding: The plan proposes an 'instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter' for /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key, but the existing iam.tf already grants ssm:GetParameter/GetParameters/GetParametersByPath on arn:aws:ssm:…:parameter/rebar/prod/* to the instance role — a prefix that covers the new parameter. The codebase's own iam_s4a.tf explicitly documents this contract: 'no new grant is required — adding one would be redundant and would dilute the single prefix grant into two overlapping statements.' Adding a separate narrower grant contradicts this established single-owner pattern and creates redundant overlapping IAM statements.
findings.location: What section, bullet 'an instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter'; AC1 ('instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter'); compare infra/terraform/iam.tf lines 50–79 and infra/terraform/iam_s4a.tf lines 13–19
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `iam_opcert.tf` documentation-anchor file (mirroring iam_s4a.tf) that references `data.aws_iam_role.instance` and explicitly states the existing /rebar/prod/* grant covers the new parameter — no new `aws_iam_role_policy` resource.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC1's 'instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter' to acknowledge the existing `/rebar/prod/*` wildcard grant (owned by S1/iam.tf) already covers the opcert key — clarify whether this story adds a redundant narrow policy for documentation purposes or accepts the existing broad grant as sufficient, so the AC reflects achievable security posture.
findings.finding: The plan states the existing instance-profile SSM grant is already 'scoped to exactly that parameter path' for the new opcert key. In practice, iam.tf's `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` policy grants `ssm:GetParameter*` on `parameter/rebar/prod/*` — a broad wildcard covering all prod secrets, not a path-exact grant. The AC claims this story adds 'an instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter,' but adding a narrower overlapping inline policy alongside the existing broad wildcard does not actually restrict access to only that parameter — the broad grant remains in effect. The 'scoped to exactly that parameter' language in the AC is therefore unachievable as written without either narrowing the existing broad policy (which this story does not own, per the SINGLE-OWNER CONTRACT in iam.tf) or accepting that 'exactly scoped' is aspirational.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter' with 'a Terraform declaration confirming the instance profile's existing /rebar/prod/* SSM read grant covers the opcert parameter path (no new broader grant is introduced).' If true least-privilege scoping is required, note that S1's iam.tf wildcard must be narrowed — a breaking change requiring coordination with the S1 owner.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete name or ARN (or naming convention) of the admin IAM role that the API Gateway SigV4 authorizer's resource-based policy restricts to, so the executor can write the Terraform principal without guessing.
findings.finding: The 'named admin role' that the SigV4 authorizer is restricted to is referenced throughout the plan and ACs but its name/ARN is never stated. An executor writing the Terraform `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource or its IAM resource-based policy must supply a concrete principal; without a stated default, they must stop and ask.
findings.location: What section — 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer, invoke permission restricted to a named admin role'; AC1 — 'restricted to a named admin role'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'The invoke-permission principal is the existing `rebar-deployer` role (ARN resolvable via `data.aws_iam_role.rebar_deployer.arn`); state the role name in the plan so the Terraform resource policy is unambiguous.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name (or define) the specific IAM role ARN/name that the API Gateway SigV4 invoke permission is restricted to — either reference an existing role by name (e.g. the operator's assumed role) or declare a new one — so the Terraform resource policy can be written with a scoped, auditable principal.
findings.finding: The 'named admin role' whose ARN/name the SigV4 IAM authorizer is restricted to is never stated anywhere in the plan. The plan says 'invoke permission restricted to a named admin role' and AC1 says 'restricted to a named admin role' but no role name or ARN is given. An implementer must invent this at writing time, which creates a concrete risk of selecting an over-broad role (e.g. `AdministratorAccess` or `*`) or an incorrect account-level role, violating least-privilege.
findings.location: What section ('invoke permission restricted to a named admin role') and AC1 ('restricted to a named admin role over TLS')
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC1, replace 'a named admin role' with the actual role name/ARN (e.g. `arn:aws:iam::896586841071:role/rebar-operator` or `arn:aws:iam::896586841071:assumed-role/AWSReservedSSO_AdministratorAccess_*/operator`) so the Terraform `aws_api_gateway_resource_policy` `Principal` can be scoped exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact name or ARN of the 'named admin role' that is granted `execute-api:Invoke` on the API Gateway (e.g., reference the existing `rebar-terraform-plan` OIDC role or name the intended caller role explicitly in the plan) so the Terraform resource-based policy can be written unambiguously.
findings.finding: The 'named admin role' that the SigV4 authorizer restricts invoke permission to is referenced throughout the plan and ACs but its identity (name/ARN) is never stated. An executor writing the API Gateway resource-based policy cannot determine the principal to restrict to, leaving the authorized-caller surface undeclared on a public-internet-facing endpoint.
findings.location: What section — 'invoke permission restricted to a named admin role'; AC line 1 — 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer restricted to a named admin role'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `var.opcert_invoker_role_arn` Terraform variable (or reference an existing named role resource) and use it as the sole principal in the API Gateway resource policy: `aws_api_gateway_rest_api_policy` / `aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource policy `Principal: {AWS: var.opcert_invoker_role_arn}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact origin-guard HTTP header name (e.g. `X-Rebar-Origin-Guard`) so Terraform's integration parameter and the nginx `if` directive use the same string without requiring executor invention.
findings.finding: The origin-guard header name is never specified. Both the API Gateway integration (which must inject it) and the nginx config (which must reject requests missing it) need the exact header name. An executor must either invent a name or stop and ask.
findings.location: What section — 'injecting a shared origin-guard header'; compose section — 'the origin-guard header value'; AC3 — 'the nginx route requires the origin-guard header'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The origin-guard header is named `X-Rebar-Origin-Guard`; its value is a random secret seeded as the compose env var `REBAR_OPCERT_ORIGIN_GUARD`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that the existing `/rebar/prod/*` instance-profile SSM read grant already covers the opcert key path — the new grant is additive documentation only, not an effective restriction — and that the key's primary isolation is the SigV4 gate, not a new narrow IAM boundary.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the instance-profile grant will be 'scoped to exactly that parameter path' (/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key), implying tight least-privilege isolation. However, the existing iam.tf policy 'rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read' already grants ssm:GetParameter* on 'arn:aws:ssm:region:account:parameter/rebar/prod/*' (the entire /rebar/prod/ namespace). IAM grants are evaluated as a union, so the new single-path grant adds zero restriction — the opcert key is already reachable by the instance role via the existing wildcard. The scoping claim is technically correct as a new addition but misleading as an isolation guarantee.
findings.location: What section — 'an instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter path'; AC1 — 'instance-profile read grant scoped to exactly that parameter'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the TLS posture of the API Gateway → EC2 origin integration leg: either confirm the integration URL uses HTTPS (nginx already has a Let's Encrypt cert on the host) and specify the backend URL as `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/`, or note that the integration uses HTTP to loopback (127.0.0.1) which would require a VPC-internal path — the plan must state which and why it is safe.
findings.finding: The plan states 'TLS only' for the API Gateway endpoint but describes the backend integration as 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin'. API Gateway HTTP API integrations call a URL; if that URL is plain HTTP to the EC2 box's public IP (rather than HTTPS or a VPC-internal path), the origin-guard header and request body traverse an untrusted network segment in plaintext between API Gateway's edge and the EC2 instance, defeating the confidentiality of the guard header.
findings.location: What section — 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the /opcert/ routes, injecting a shared origin-guard header'
findings.suggested_fix: Set the API Gateway HTTP API integration URL to `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` (the host already has a valid TLS cert via Let's Encrypt per the existing nginx setup) so the full request path — client → API GW (TLS) → EC2 origin (TLS) — is encrypted end-to-end.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3's test assertion to include the origin-guard header value env var in the compose `opcert` service environment, so all three described env entries (REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID, SSM parameter name, origin-guard header value) are verified.
findings.finding: AC3's test-assertion scope omits the origin-guard header value env var that the What section explicitly requires in the compose service's environment.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; What section (Compose paragraph)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: '…the env wiring (`REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, SSM parameter name, and origin-guard header value)…' so the test covers all three env entries described in the What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the merge-gate consumer cited as '4214' in the Scope/Out section is confirmed shipped (e.g. link to the merged change-set or a codebase grep for its implementation) so the scope exclusion is grounded rather than asserted.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope/Out section asserts 'the merge-gate consumer (shipped, 4214)' is already shipped and therefore excluded from this story's scope. No file, symbol, or code reference to ticket/story '4214' was found anywhere in the repository. This is a scope-exclusion claim that cannot be grounded in the codebase, meaning a false exclusion could invisibly delete work (the consumer integration) that is actually needed for this story.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Out: … the merge-gate consumer (shipped, 4214)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of ACs 1, 3, and 4, anchor the proving approach to a concrete command type (e.g. `terraform show -json | jq -e` for AC1, `python -m pytest infra/tests/test_compose.py` with a named assertion for AC3, `python -c 'import yaml; …assert entry["env_id"]…'` for AC4) so reviewers can judge fixture validity.
findings.finding: ACs 1, 3, and 4 all say 'a test asserts X' without specifying the test type, file location, or proving command, leaving the executor free to implement a trivially-passing grep (substring false-positive, defect #1) or a fixture that doesn't structurally validate the claim (defect #4).
findings.location: AC1 — 'A test asserts these declarations exist'; AC3 — 'a test asserts the service entry, the env wiring … and that the nginx route requires the origin-guard header'; AC4 — 'a test asserts the entry parses and the `env_id` matches'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0048-opcert-sigv4-gateway-origin-guard.md`) documenting the zero-fixed-cost constraint, the SigV4+nginx-origin-guard pattern, and the explicit rejection of Fargate/Secrets Manager/KMS-CMK, and add it to the Scope 'In' list.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — SigV4-gated API Gateway HTTP API fronting an existing EC2 box via an nginx origin-guard header, with an SSM-stored passphrase-free Ed25519 key — but does not declare a new ADR for this design. The project already has a numbered ADR tradition (ADR 0046, ADR 0047 referenced in-plan). The 'epic design resolution' is cited but is not confirmed to be a numbered ADR or formal decision record.
findings.location: Why section (ADR 0046/0047 references); What section (architecture description); Scope section (no ADR in-scope)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0048-opcert-sigv4-gateway-ssm-key.md` capturing: (1) context — zero new fixed monthly cost constraint; (2) decision — API Gateway HTTP API + IAM SigV4 + nginx origin-guard header + SSM SecureString for Ed25519 key; (3) rejected alternatives — Fargate+NLB+Secrets Manager+KMS CMK and why; (4) consequences — key seeded out-of-band, SigV4 as the only authenticated path. Reference this ADR from the Terraform and compose configs via a comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and mitigate the API-Gateway ↔ nginx origin-guard header coupling: define the header name and value in a single Terraform local (or SSM parameter) referenced by both the API GW integration config and the compose/nginx config, and extend the AC-3 test to assert both sides reference the same source-of-truth value.
findings.finding: The origin-guard header is a security-critical shared secret between API Gateway (injected at the integration layer) and nginx (enforced at the route level), constituting a silent cross-component contract. The plan describes both sides functionally but does not acknowledge or mitigate the coupling risk: if the API Gateway integration header and the nginx rejection rule diverge (e.g., header name/value changed in one place), the security control silently breaks with no test catching it end-to-end before deployment.
findings.location: What section — 'injecting a shared origin-guard header'; 'an nginx route /opcert/ → the service that rejects requests missing the origin-guard header'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: In Terraform, define `locals { opcert_origin_guard_header = { name = "X-Rebar-Origin-Guard", value = random_password.origin_guard.result } }` and reference it in both the API Gateway integration `request_parameters` and as the compose env `REBAR_OPCERT_ORIGIN_GUARD_HEADER`. The test in AC-3 can then assert the nginx config's required header value matches the compose env var, closing the coupling gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability specification: define a container health-check in the compose service entry, enable API Gateway access logging to CloudWatch, and specify at least one alarm (e.g., 5xx rate or container restart count) so operators can detect and debug opcert failures.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation is specified for the new failure points: the opcert compose service (container health, request errors) and the API Gateway integration (5xx rate, latency) are introduced with no mention of logging configuration, metrics emission, or alerting, leaving operators blind to failures in production.
findings.location: What section / Compose and API Gateway wiring; AC item 3 (compose/nginx)
findings.suggested_fix: In the compose service definition add a `healthcheck` stanza (e.g., HTTP GET /health → opcert's health endpoint). In the Terraform module, enable `access_log_settings` on the API Gateway stage pointing to a CloudWatch log group, and add a `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` on `5XXError` count. Reference these in the relevant AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify startup resilience for SSM reads in the opcert service: define a retry-with-backoff policy (e.g., up to N attempts with exponential delay) for the initial GetParameter call, and document that the service exits non-zero (triggering compose `restart: on-failure`) rather than silently swallowing the error.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify retry, timeout, or graceful-degradation behavior when the SSM Parameter Store is unavailable at opcert service startup; a transient SSM outage will permanently prevent the container from starting (or cause an unhandled crash), with no recovery path short of a manual restart.
findings.location: What section — SSM SecureString + instance-profile grant; Deploy+prove section
findings.suggested_fix: Add `restart: on-failure` (or `unless-stopped`) to the compose service entry. In the sibling-story service spec (or reference it here), require that SSM fetch retries with exponential backoff (e.g., 3 attempts, 1s/2s/4s) and logs the error before exiting, so compose will automatically re-attempt.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure to the plan (e.g., in the Deploy+prove section or a dedicated Rollback subsection): specify the command sequence to undo each step (compose service stop → terraform destroy targeting only the new resources → SSM parameter deletion → revert trusted_environments.yaml commit), and note that re-seeding a new key requires regenerating and re-pinning the public key.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no explicit rollback procedure; if the terraform apply, compose deploy, or key-seeding step partially succeeds or produces a broken deployment, there is no documented cheap path to undo the change — particularly the SSM key seeding, which is not idempotent (re-seeding with a new key invalidates the pinned public key in trusted_environments.yaml).
findings.location: Deploy+prove section; AC item 5 (operator-attested)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a key rotation and retirement lifecycle for the SSM SecureString and trusted_environments.yaml: specify the ordered steps to rotate (generate new key → update SSM → commit new public key to yaml → remove old entry), identify who owns the operation, and add a RETIRE step (delete SSM parameter + remove yaml entry + commit) for environment decommission.
findings.finding: The lifecycle for the SSM SecureString Ed25519 private key and the corresponding trusted_environments.yaml public-key entry has no defined key-rotation or retirement path; if the key is compromised or the environment is decommissioned, there is no specified procedure to replace the key, update the SSM parameter, and re-pin the public key atomically, leaving a window where the old and new keys are inconsistent.
findings.location: What section — SSM SecureString declaration; AC item 4 (trusted_environments.yaml pinning)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` as an explicit edit target (new `/opcert/` location block with origin-guard header enforcement), and clarify whether the origin-guard header value enters the template via `envsubst` (requiring an update to the envsubst invocation in the deploy flow) or is a compile-time literal.
findings.finding: `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` is a concrete edit target required by the plan (the `/opcert/` route with origin-guard header enforcement is an nginx-level change) but is never named in the What section or ACs as an artifact to be modified.
findings.location: What section ('Compose — extend … nginx route `/opcert/`'); AC3 ('nginx config gains the `opcert` service and the guarded `/opcert/` route')
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add a bullet: 'nginx (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`): add a `/opcert/` location block that forwards to the opcert container port and rejects (403) any request missing the `X-Rebar-Origin-Guard: <value>` header; if the header value is environment-specific, extend the `envsubst` variable list in the boot/deploy script.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the `opcert` compose service requires any `external: true` named volume, and if so, add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` (extend `SITE_SUBDIRS`) as an edit target to satisfy the config-check.sh check-5 drift gate.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is an unenumerated consumer: any `external: true` named volume added for the `opcert` service must be reflected in its `SITE_SUBDIRS` list, or config-check.sh check 5 (the incident-2731 drift gate) will fail CI.
findings.location: What section ('Compose — extend `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`'); Scope section (In: 'compose/nginx wiring')
findings.suggested_fix: If the service is stateless (reads SSM directly, no persistent volume needed), add a note: 'The `opcert` service requires no `external: true` volume (stateless; key read from SSM at runtime), so `compose-up.sh`/`SITE_SUBDIRS` requires no change.' If a volume is needed, name it and list `compose-up.sh` as an edit target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that for API Gateway HTTP API, AWS_IAM/SigV4 auth is set via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` resource (not via a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource, which only accepts JWT/Lambda types) — and that the invoke-permission restriction to a named admin role is expressed as an IAM resource policy (`aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource policy) scoped to that role's ARN.
findings.finding: For API Gateway HTTP API (V2), AWS_IAM / SigV4 authorization is NOT a discrete authorizer resource — it is set via authorization_type = 'AWS_IAM' on each aws_apigatewayv2_route resource. The aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer resource only supports JWT and Lambda authorizer types for HTTP APIs. The plan's language 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' implies a dedicated authorizer resource, which does not exist for this auth type in the Terraform AWS provider and would produce a plan error if coded that way.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer'; AC1 — 'IAM SigV4 authorizer'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with: 'AWS_IAM authorization set on each aws_apigatewayv2_route (authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"), plus an aws_apigatewayv2_api resource policy restricting execute-api:Invoke to the named admin role ARN — no aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer resource is used (that resource supports only JWT/Lambda types for HTTP APIs).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for the new infra: at minimum, specify an API Gateway access log group + a CloudWatch alarm on APIGW 5xx rate or integration errors, and a Docker `healthcheck` on the opcert compose service; also document the teardown procedure (terraform destroy of the APIGW resource, deletion of the SSM parameter, and removal of the entry from `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`).
findings.finding: No observability (logging, metrics, or alarms) is specified for the two new failure points introduced by this story: the API Gateway HTTP API (4xx/5xx error rate, integration latency, throttle) and the opcert compose service (container health, request error rate). The existing monitoring covers the EC2 host and Gerrit reachability but does not cover the APIGW integration layer or the opcert service directly. Additionally, no teardown procedure is described for decommissioning the API Gateway and removing the pinned public key from `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`.
findings.location: What section, Acceptance Criteria (all ACs), Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's Terraform description: for API Gateway HTTP API, AWS_IAM/SigV4 auth is declared via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` (not via a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource, which only accepts `JWT`/`REQUEST` types for HTTP APIs).
findings.finding: For API Gateway HTTP API (V2), AWS_IAM/SigV4 authorization is NOT a discrete authorizer resource — it is set via authorization_type = "AWS_IAM" on each aws_apigatewayv2_route resource. The aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer Terraform resource only accepts authorizer_type = "JWT" or "REQUEST" (Lambda) for HTTP APIs; specifying AWS_IAM as an authorizer_type is invalid and causes terraform validate to fail. The plan's language 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' implies a dedicated authorizer resource that does not exist for this auth type in the HTTP API model.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer'; AC1 — 'IAM SigV4 authorizer restricted to a named admin role'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with: 'each `/opcert/` route configured with `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on the `aws_apigatewayv2_route` resource (HTTP API IAM auth is route-level, not a discrete authorizer resource)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
